summary: Adversarially audit ChatGPT claimed 14-stroke squaring-the-circle construction for correctness, legality, fair counting, and its 1/44^4 extension

findings.claim: Final |IW| = sqrt(355/113), i.e. IW^2 = 355/113 exactly
findings.detail: Independent recomputation (mpmath, 50 dps, /tmp/audit-chatgpt14/verify.py, no hardcoded claimed coordinates) gives |IW| = 1.77245392615830 (15 digits; full: 1.772453926158302796091946...). |IW| - sqrt(355/113) = 0 to all 50 digits (IW^2 - 355/113 = -5.3e-51, pure roundoff). |IW| - sqrt(pi) = +7.525278677e-8; relative side error (IW-sqrt(pi))/sqrt(pi) = +4.245683843e-8, matching the claimed +4.246e-8 (Ramanujan-1913 accuracy: his construction also yields side sqrt(355/113)).

findings.claim: All intermediate points as claimed: W=(0,sqrt3), Q=(0,1), X=(1/2,0), G=(7/8,0), K=(112/113,98/113), H=(4/sqrt113, 7/(2 sqrt113)) with |OH|=1/2, I=(4/sqrt113,0)
findings.detail: Every point recomputed from generic circle-circle / line-circle / line-line routines matches the claimed coordinates to 50 digits (all |diff| = 0). Supporting derivation also verified: power equality g^2-r^2=(r-g)^2-(r/2)^2 holds exactly at g=7/8; radical-axis line OK is perpendicular to QG (dot product = 0) with slope exactly 7/8; |XH|^2 = 1/2 - 4/sqrt(113) exactly, which is what forces the step-6 chord to be the vertical line x = 4/sqrt(113) through H and its mirror H'; K is outside GAMMA at |OK| = 1.31700921... in the upper right, as the figure claims.

findings.claim: Every intersection used exists, with two distinct points where two are needed
findings.detail: All existence conditions verified strictly. Circle-circle (|r1-r2| < d < r1+r2, disc h^2 > 0): c(P,PB)^c(B,PB) d=2, radii 2,2, h^2=3; GAMMA^c(B,BO) d=1, radii 1,1, h^2=0.75; GAMMA^c(B,BX) d=1, radii 1,0.5, h^2=0.234375; c(G,GO)^c(Q,QO) d=sqrt(113)/8=1.32877, radii 0.875,1, h^2=0.43363 (one intersection is O to within 1.5e-51, so 'K other than O' is well-posed); c(X,XH)^c(O,OX) d=0.5, radii 0.35173,0.5, h^2=0.10841. Line-circle (disc > 0): OW^GAMMA disc=12; OK^c(O,OX) disc=1.7345. Line-line: the three chord-vs-OB intersections X, G, I all have nonzero determinant (chords are vertical, OB horizontal) and land strictly inside segment OB (x = 0.5, 0.875, 0.3763), so even a segment reading of the diameter suffices.

findings.claim: Prose ambiguities about which intersection to take are all resolved by the figure hints
findings.detail: Four genuinely two-valued choices: (1) W = c(P,PB)^c(B,PB) has points (0,+/-sqrt3) — 'upper per figure' resolves; (2) Q = OW^GAMMA has (0,+/-1) — 'upper' resolves; (3) K — 'other than O' is fully unambiguous, no figure needed; (4) H = OK^c(O,OX) has two antipodal points — prose alone is ambiguous, but 'toward K / upper right' resolves. X, G, I are unique (line-line). Moreover, an exhaustive sweep of all 8 sign combinations (W upper/lower x Q upper/lower x H toward/away) gives the identical |IW| = 1.772453926158302796... in every case, because I always lands at x = +/-4/sqrt(113) on the x-axis while W sits on the y-axis — so the construction is robust even if a reader picks the 'wrong' branch.

findings.claim: Exact 14-stroke count under the stated metric
findings.detail: Recount: (1) c(P,PB), (2) c(B,PB), (3) line OW, (4) c(B,BO), (5) c(Q,QO), (6) common-chord line of GAMMA & c(B,BO), (7) c(O,OX), (8) c(B,BX), (9) common-chord line of GAMMA & c(B,BX), (10) c(G,GO), (11) line OK, (12) c(X,XH), (13) common-chord line of c(X,XH) & c(O,OX), (14) segment IW. Total = 14, matching the per-step tally 3+2+2+2+2+2+1. Nothing is drawn but unused: every stroke feeds a later intersection or is the answer segment. Each common-chord line is legitimately drawable with a straightedge because the two defining circle intersections exist (verified) and marking them is free under the stated metric.

findings.claim: Used but never drawn: the line OB (the x-axis)
findings.detail: Steps 3, 4 and 6 intersect chord lines with 'OB' to get X, G, I, but no stroke is ever spent on line OB. The count is honest only under the reading that the given 'diameter P-O-B' is an already-drawn line, not just three marked points. That reading is conventional and is arguably what the problem statement says, but it is load-bearing: with bare points P, O, B as givens, drawing line PB costs one more stroke and the total becomes 15. Mitigating: X (0.5), G (0.875), I (0.3763) all lie strictly between O and B, so the given diameter segment (not an extension) suffices.

sim.summary: Wrote an independent 50-digit mpmath simulation (/tmp/audit-chatgpt14/verify.py) of the prose construction using only generic circle-circle, line-circle, and line-line intersection routines — claimed coordinates were used only as post-hoc checks. Result: the construction is EXACT. |IW| = 1.77245392615830 (15 digits), equal to sqrt(355/113) to all 50 computed digits; |IW| - sqrt(pi) = +7.5253e-8, relative side error +4.2457e-8, matching the claimed +4.246e-8 and Ramanujan's 1913 accuracy. Every claimed intermediate point (W, Q, X, G, K, H, I) reproduces exactly; every intersection exists with strict margin (all discriminants positive, all circle pairs satisfy |r1-r2| < d < r1+r2); K 'other than O' is well-posed (the other common point is O to 1.5e-51). The four branch ambiguities (W, Q, H two-valued; K unambiguous) are all settled by the figure hints, and an exhaustive 8-way sweep shows the final length is invariant under every branch choice anyway. Stroke recount confirms exactly 14 strokes with nothing drawn-but-unused. Single caveat: line OB is used three times (to locate X, G, I) but never drawn — legitimate only if the given 'diameter P-O-B' counts as a pre-drawn line; under a bare-points reading the count is 15. All three intersection points fall inside segment OB, so the given diameter segment itself suffices.

findings.claim: W = (0, sqrt(3)) from c(P,PB) and c(B,PB); OW = sqrt(3); Q = (0,1)
findings.detail: With P=(-1,0), B=(1,0), radius |PB|=2, sympy gives intersections exactly (0, ±sqrt(3)). Upper point W=(0,sqrt(3)), |OW|=sqrt(3) exactly (equilateral-triangle altitude on base 2). Line OW is the y-axis; it meets the unit circle GAMMA at (0,±1), upper Q=(0,1).

findings.claim: Common chord of GAMMA and c(B,BO) is x = 1/2, so X = (1/2, 0)
findings.detail: GAMMA ∩ c(B,1) = {(1/2, ±sqrt(3)/2)}, both real and distinct; the chord line has equation x - 1/2 = 0 and meets OB at exactly (1/2,0). Subtracting the circle equations: x^2 - (x-1)^2 = 0 gives x = 1/2.

findings.claim: Common chord of GAMMA and c(B,BX) is x = 7/8, so G = (7/8, 0)
findings.detail: c(B,BX) has radius |BX| = 1/2. GAMMA ∩ c(B,1/2) = {(7/8, ±sqrt(15)/8)}, real and distinct (center distance 1 lies strictly between |1 - 1/2| and 1 + 1/2, so the circles genuinely intersect). Chord is x = 7/8, meeting OB at G = (7/8, 0).

findings.claim: Power-of-a-point derivation g^2 - r^2 = (r-g)^2 - (r/2)^2 => g = 7r/8 is sound, signs included
findings.detail: The common chord lies on the radical axis, where signed powers (|PC|^2 - rho^2) are equal. LHS is the power of (g,0) w.r.t. GAMMA, RHS w.r.t. c(B, r/2); solving symbolically gives the unique solution g = 7r/8. Sign check: at G both powers equal -15/64 (G is inside both circles), consistent and equal. Note (r-g)^2 = (g-r)^2 = |GB|^2, so the ordering g < r is harmless. Argument fully sound.

findings.claim: c(G,GO) and c(Q,QO) meet at O and K = (112/113, 98/113); line OK perpendicular to QG, direction (8,7)
findings.detail: Both circles pass through O; sympy gives intersections exactly {(0,0), (112/113, 98/113)}. Not tangent at O (Q, G, O non-collinear), so K is a genuine second point. Subtracting the equations gives radical axis 7x = 8y, i.e. direction (8,7), and (8,7)·(G-Q) = (8,7)·(7/8,-1) = 0, so OK ⊥ QG. Direction (1, 7/8) in their derivation is the same as (8,7).

Line OK passes through the center O, so it is a diameter of c(O,1/2); intersections are ±(1/2)(8,7)/sqrt(113). Upper-right choice is exactly (4/sqrt(113), 7/(2 sqrt(113))) = (4 sqrt(113)/113, 7 sqrt(113)/226), with |OH| = 1/2 verified symbolically.

findings.claim: Common chord of c(X,XH) and c(O,OX) is the vertical line x = 4/sqrt(113); I = (4/sqrt(113), 0)
findings.detail: Both centers (X=(1/2,0) and O) lie on the x-axis; both circles pass through H (c(X,XH) by construction, c(O,OX) because |OH| = 1/2 = OX). Intersections are symmetric about the line of centers, so they meet at H and its mirror H'; sympy confirms both intersection points have x = 4 sqrt(113)/113 exactly. Chord is vertical through H, meeting OB at I = (4/sqrt(113), 0). H is off-axis (y = 7 sqrt(113)/226 ≠ 0) so two distinct intersections exist.

findings.claim: Angle IOW = 90 degrees exactly; IW^2 = 3 + 16/113 = 355/113 exactly
findings.detail: OI lies on the positive x-axis, OW on the positive y-axis; dot product is 0 symbolically. IW^2 = |I - W|^2 = 16/113 + 3 = 355/113 exactly (sympy: simplify(IW^2 - 355/113) = 0). So IW = r sqrt(355/113), i.e. the construction encodes the pi-approximation 355/113.

findings.claim: Relative side error +4.246e-8, same as Ramanujan 1913
findings.detail: sqrt(355/113)/sqrt(pi) - 1 = +4.2456838e-8 (positive since 355/113 > pi); relative area error = +8.4913679e-8. Ramanujan's 1913 J. Indian Math. Soc. construction also produces a side of r sqrt(355/113), so the accuracy is identical by construction. The rounded figure 4.246e-8 is correct.

findings.claim: 14 strokes under the stated metric
findings.detail: 3+2+2+2+2+2+1 = 14 with GAMMA and diameter POB given. Every 'common-chord line' stroke is legitimate: in each case the two intersection points were shown to be real and distinct, and both defining circles are already drawn, so one straightedge stroke suffices. Caveat (convention, not error): the metric is generous — 'compass settings free' assumes a non-collapsing compass (e.g. transferring |XH| in c(X,XH) is free), and intersections cost nothing. Under classical collapsing-compass accounting the count would be higher, but the claim is internally consistent with its own stated metric.

geom.summary: Every mathematical claim in the 14-stroke construction verifies exactly under symbolic computation (sympy, exact rationals/radicals, r = 1 WLOG since all lengths scale). The chain W=(0,sqrt(3)) -> X=(1/2,0) -> G=(7/8,0) -> K=(112/113,98/113) -> H=(4/sqrt(113), 7/(2 sqrt(113))) -> I=(4/sqrt(113),0) is exact at every step; all circle intersections used are real, distinct, and non-tangent; the power-of-a-point derivation g = 7r/8 is sound including signs (both powers equal -15/64 at G, and the common chord lies on the radical axis where equality is the defining condition). The final segment satisfies IW^2 = 355/113 r^2 exactly because OI ⊥ OW (I on the x-axis, W on the y-axis). Numerically, the relative side error vs the true circle-squaring side r sqrt(pi) is +4.2456838e-8 (area error +8.4913679e-8), matching the claimed +4.246e-8 and identical to Ramanujan's 1913 construction, which encodes the same approximation pi ≈ 355/113. The stroke count of 14 is correct under the stated metric; the only soft spot is that the metric itself is generous (non-collapsing compass, free distance transfer, free intersections), which is a bookkeeping convention rather than a mathematical error. No claim could be refuted.

findings.claim: All eight circles are type (b) collapsing-legal (centered at a constructed point THROUGH another constructed point); zero rigid transfers
findings.detail: c(P,PB): center P through B — (b). c(B,PB): radius segment PB has the center B as an endpoint, so it is simply the circle centered B through P — (b), not a transfer. c(B,BO): center B through O — (b). c(Q,QO): center Q through O — (b). c(O,OX): center O through X — (b). c(B,BX): center B through X — (b). c(G,GO): center G through O — (b). c(X,XH): center X through H — (b). In every case the radius segment is incident to the center; the compass is never lifted with a stored setting. All six lines (OW, chord step 3, chord step 4, OK, chord step 6, IW) are type (a): each passes through two previously constructed points (the three common-chord lines pass through the two circle-circle intersection points, which are free). Stroke tally is honest: 8 circles + 6 lines = 14 = 3+2+2+2+2+2+1.

findings.claim: Dependency order is sound: no point used before constructed; chords only drawn between already-drawn circles; H exists before c(X,XH)
findings.detail: Step 1 uses only givens P, B, O. Step 3 chord uses GAMMA (given) and c(B,BO) (step 2). Step 4 draws c(B,BX) BEFORE its chord with GAMMA. Step 5 intersects c(G,GO) with c(Q,QO) (drawn step 2); both pass through O by construction, so K = second common point is well-defined; H = OK ∩ c(O,OX) uses c(O,OX) from step 3. Step 6 uses X (step 3) and H (step 5); its chord with c(O,OX) passes through H and its mirror H' (both circles have centers on the x-axis, hence symmetric, hence share H'), both free intersection points of two drawn circles. Step 7 uses I (step 6) and W (step 1). One prerequisite worth stating: X, G, I are obtained as intersections with the GIVEN diameter line P-O-B — the count of 14 depends on the diameter being given as a drawn line, not just three points. The problem statement gives it (as does Ramanujan's 1913 original), so this is legitimate, but a hostile reading that gives only the points P, O, B would cost a 15th stroke.

findings.claim: Every intersection exists (nondegenerate)
findings.detail: S1: d=2, r1=r2=2, 0<2<4 → W=(0,±√3), two points. OW ∩ GAMMA: line through center, always two points; Q=(0,1). S3: GAMMA vs c(B,1): d=1, 0<1<2 → (1/2,±√3/2). S4: GAMMA vs c(B,1/2): d=1, 1/2<1<3/2 → (7/8,±√15/8), y²=15/64>0. S5 circles: d=|QG|=√113/8≈1.3288, |7/8−1|=0.125 < d < 1.875 → two points O and K=(112/113,98/113) (verified exactly on both circles); |OK|²=196/113≈1.7345>1 so K is outside GAMMA as claimed. OK ∩ c(O,OX): line through center → H=(4/√113, 7/(2√113)), |OH|=1/2 exactly; K is even outside c(O,OX), so segment OK crosses it exactly once — H is unique without appeal to the figure. S6: |XH|²=1/2−4/√113≈0.12371 (verified exactly: (4/√113−1/2)²+49/452, with 64/452+49/452=1/4); d=|OX|=1/2, |1/2−0.35175|≈0.148<1/2<0.852 → two points H, H'. Radical axis x = 1/2−|XH|² = 4/√113 → I=(4/√113,0)≈0.37629, inside (0,1). All chord lines are vertical, the diameter horizontal — no parallel degeneracy. Right angle at O is by construction (W on perpendicular bisector of PB, which passes through midpoint O), so IW²=OI²+OW²=16/113+3=355/113 exactly. Side = √(355/113)r, relative error +4.2457e-8 vs √π — matches the claimed +4.246e-8 and equals Ramanujan 1913's value exactly.

findings.claim: The claimed metric is equivalent to the reference metric under the natural reading, and the construction never exploits 'compass settings are free'
findings.detail: 'Compass settings are free' = rigid compass with uncharged radius transfer, which is exactly the reference metric ('circle with constructed center and radius = distance between two constructed points = 1'); 'intersections free' is standard in both. One literal-wording quibble: 'one complete line or circle = one stroke' as written omits the anchoring requirement (it does not say the line must pass through two constructed points or the circle have a constructed center/radius), so a rules lawyer could abuse the letter of it to draw unconstructible lines (tangents, neusis) at cost 1 — literally LOOSER than the reference. But the construction never uses that slack: every line is through two constructed points and every circle is center-through-constructed-point, which satisfies not just the reference metric but the strictly tighter collapsing metric. The permission to transfer distances is granted and then never used.

findings.claim: 14 strokes stand unchanged under BOTH modern rigid-compass rules and strict Euclid collapsing-compass rules
findings.detail: (i) Rigid: legal, 14, honest. (ii) Strict collapsing: since all eight circles are Postulate-3-direct (place needle on constructed center, pencil on constructed point already at the correct distance), Euclid I.2 compass-carrying is needed NOWHERE, so the count is 14 under collapsing rules too — no expansion. Niceties checked and all benign: even under a segment-only Postulate-1 reading, no Postulate-2 'produce' is ever required — Q lies between O and W (1<√3); X, G, I each lie between the two chord endpoints that define their lines (chords are vertical, symmetric about the axis); H lies between O and K (0.5<1.317); X=1/2, G=7/8, I≈0.376 all lie strictly inside the given segment O-B (and within diameter P-B). The only figure-dependent choices are the upper branch for W and Q and the K-side intersection for H (the latter actually forced if one reads 'H on segment OK'), and 'other intersection' for K is unambiguous — all selection, not strokes, free under both metrics.

findings.claim: Supporting derivation is internally correct
findings.detail: OW=√3 (equilateral altitude on base PB=2) ✓. OX=1/2 via GAMMA/c(B,BO) radical axis ✓. Power equality g²−r²=(r−g)²−(r/2)² gives g=7/8 ✓ (and matches direct radical-axis subtraction x=7/8). Line OK is the radical axis of c(Q,QO) and c(G,GO) because both O and K lie on both circles; its direction (8,7)∝(1,7/8) is perpendicular to QG=(7/8,−1) (dot product 0) ✓. OI=(1/2)/√(1+49/64)=4/√113 ✓. IW²=3+16/113=355/113 ✓, the Ramanujan 355/113 approximation to π.

legality.summary: No violation found, despite an adversarial audit. All 14 strokes are legal and honestly counted: 8 circles + 6 lines = 14, matching the per-step tally 3+2+2+2+2+2+1. Crucially, all eight circles are collapsing-legal type (b) — in every case (including c(B,PB), whose radius segment PB is incident to the center B) the compass pivots on a constructed center with the pencil on an already-constructed point at the desired distance — so there are ZERO rigid transfers and Euclid I.2 is never needed; the 14-stroke count stands unchanged under strict collapsing-compass rules. Dependency order is clean (every chord drawn only after both circles exist; H before c(X,XH)); every intersection was verified nondegenerate by the two-circle criterion or line-through-center argument, with exact coordinates confirming X=(1/2,0), G=(7/8,0), K=(112/113,98/113) with |OK|²=196/113>1 (outside GAMMA), |OH|=1/2, |XH|²=1/2−4/√113, I=(4/√113,0), and IW²=355/113 exactly; relative side error +4.2457e-8 matches the claim and Ramanujan 1913. The claimed metric ('settings free') is a rigid-compass metric equivalent to the reference under the natural reading — literally slightly looser as worded (no anchoring clause) — but the construction never exploits the slack, using only moves legal under the tighter collapsing metric. Only two niceties: (1) the count relies on the given diameter P-O-B being a drawn line (as stated in the givens; a points-only reading would cost a 15th stroke); (2) no segment ever needs producing — Q, X, G, H, I all fall strictly inside the segments that define their lines, and every branch choice (W, Q upper; H toward K) is selection, not a stroke.

findings.detail: 44^2 = 1936, 1936^2 = 44^4 = 3,748,096 exactly. At 60 digits, q* = 3.141592653551851298904216731653684... Every quoted digit matches (next digits are 042..., so the quoted truncation is also a correct rounding). Exact fraction: q* = 1330573967/423534848.

findings.claim: Relative side error sqrt(q*/pi) - 1 = -6.0386472e-12, with q* < pi
findings.detail: Computed sqrt(q*/pi) - 1 = -6.038647231235758814823968e-12; the quoted -6.0386472e-12 is correctly rounded to 8 significant figures. Sign check passes: q* - pi = -3.79419e-11 < 0, so q* < pi and the side error is indeed negative.

findings.claim: The extension's side error is 'about 7031 times smaller' than Ramanujan's 4.246e-8
findings.detail: Ramanujan 355/113 side error sqrt((355/113)/pi) - 1 = +4.2456838e-8 (quoted 4.246e-8 rounds correctly). Exact ratio 4.2456838e-8 / 6.0386472e-12 = 7030.85; using the quoted rounded numbers gives 7031.4. 'About 7031' is accurate.

findings.claim: Construction algebra: BG = r/8, BD = 11r/2, ratio (1/8)/(11/2) = 1/44 applied twice gives r/1936, and sqrt(IW^2 - (r/1936)^2) = sqrt(q*)·r
findings.detail: All exact: B=(1,0), G=(7/8,0) gives BG = r/8; (1/8)/(11/2) = 1/44; (1/44)^2 = 1/1936; (r/1936)^2 = r^2/3748096 = r^2/44^4. Given IW = sqrt(355/113)·r (the base construction's square side), the right-triangle leg sqrt(IW^2 - (r/1936)^2) = sqrt(355/113 - 1/44^4)·r = sqrt(q*)·r, verified symbolically (Fraction arithmetic) and numerically at 50 digits. q* is rational, so sqrt(q*) is straightedge-and-compass constructible in principle.

findings.claim: Constructible in at most 28 strokes total (14 additional beyond the 14 base strokes)
findings.detail: Tight-to-optimistic, not demonstrated as described. My explicit expansion of their LITERAL route (step off D at 11r/2: ~4 circles using 2r, 2r, r, r/2 spans; auxiliary ray through B + mark BE = r: 2; intercept-theorem 1/44 application = transversal line + 3-stroke parallel through G, done TWICE: 8; right-triangle finish: 2-6) gives ~16-18 additional strokes, i.e. 30-32 total. A total of exactly 28 IS attainable, but only via an optimization they did not state: after getting BF = r/44 (10 strokes incl. building D), draw circle center B radius r/44 meeting the base circle at Z (1 stroke), then circle center Z radius ZB re-cuts the diameter at B' with BB' = 2·(r/44)^2/(2r) = r/1936 exactly (1 stroke, chord-reflection/altitude trick — verified numerically: intersection at x = 0.99948347... = 1 - 1/1936); then circle center O radius r/1936 marks U on the perpendicular diameter (1) and circle center U radius IW cuts the main diameter at V with OV = sqrt(q*)·r (1, verified to 45 digits). That totals 14 additional = 28, but requires the base figure to already contain an r/2 span, a perpendicular diameter, and an off-axis point (each missing item costs +3-4 strokes; realistic worst case ~34-36). Count also stops at producing the side segment, not drawing the square. Verdict: 28 is credible as a best case with a smarter route, optimistic as literally described.

findings.claim: 1/44^4 is a principled correction to 355/113
findings.detail: Lucky numerical coincidence, not principled. 355/113 - pi = 2.6676418906e-7 while 1/44^4 = 2.6680213100e-7; equivalently 1/(355/113 - pi) = 3,748,629.09 vs 44^4 = 3,748,096 — off by 533 (relative mismatch 1.42e-4). No continued-fraction or series structure produces 44^4 here; it is a searched near-hit that cancels 99.9858% of the error. Notably, Ramanujan's own 1913 footnote correction pi ≈ (355/113)(1 - 0.0003/3533) is far better: its error is +1.0901e-15 (side error +1.73e-16), making it ~34,800 times more accurate than q* (error -3.7942e-11). The needed fractional correction is 8.4913671448e-8 vs Ramanujan's 0.0003/3533 = 8.4913671101e-8 — agreement to 8 significant figures. So the '7031x better than Ramanujan' framing compares only against 355/113 itself; against Ramanujan's own corrected value the extension loses by four orders of magnitude.

extension.summary: All numerical claims check out exactly at 50-60 digits: 44^4 = 3,748,096; q* = 355/113 - 1/44^4 = 3.1415926535518512989042... (every quoted digit correct); q* < pi; side error sqrt(q*/pi) - 1 = -6.0386472312e-12 (quoted value correctly rounded); ratio to Ramanujan's 4.2456838e-8 side error is 7030.85 ('about 7031' is right). The construction algebra is exact: (1/8)/(11/2) = 1/44 twice gives r/1936, and the right-triangle leg sqrt(IW^2 - (r/1936)^2) equals sqrt(q*)·r given IW = sqrt(355/113)·r. Two caveats: (1) the <=28-stroke count is tight-to-optimistic — my honest expansion of their literal 'apply the ratio twice' route costs ~30-32 strokes; exactly 28 is achievable only via an unstated chord-reflection trick that squares the ratio in 2 strokes, and only if the base figure supplies an r/2 span, a perpendicular diameter, and an off-axis point (they also concede r/1936 is undraftable in practice); (2) 1/44^4 is a lucky coincidence (1/(355/113 - pi) = 3,748,629 vs 44^4 = 3,748,096, off by 1.4e-4 relative), not a principled correction — and Ramanujan's own 1913 footnote (355/113)(1 - 0.0003/3533) is ~34,800x more accurate than q* (error 1.09e-15 vs 3.79e-11), so the extension only beats uncorrected 355/113, not Ramanujan's corrected value. Audit scripts: /tmp/audit-chatgpt14-ext/audit.py and /tmp/audit-chatgpt14-ext/strokes.py.

findings.claim: Q1: ChatGPT's stroke metric ('one complete line or circle = one stroke; intersections and compass settings free') is the same as the reference metric
findings.detail: For counting purposes the metrics are equivalent: the reference charges 1 move per line-through-2-constructed-points or circle-with-constructed-center-and-radius-between-two-constructed-points; 'compass settings are free' is the same rigid-compass allowance stated more loosely. The loose phrasing COULD in principle be read more generously (radius not anchored to constructed points), but no stroke exploits it: every one of the 8 circles is c(A,AZ) with the center A an endpoint of its own radius segment, so all radii are center-to-constructed-point distances. The wording difference does not favor ChatGPT in this construction. All chord/common-chord lines pass through 2 genuine constructed intersection points (verified numerically that every circle pair actually intersects, incl. the near-tangent cases), so every line stroke is also legal under the stricter reference wording.

findings.claim: Q2: 'Ramanujan's published chain, explicitly expanded <= 33' is a fair characterization
findings.detail: The number itself is defensible: 33 sits inside the reference's faithful/textbook band of 31-38. But it is not the apples-to-apples comparator. ChatGPT's own 14 is a maximally-reused count (e.g. c(Q,QO) drawn in step 2 and consumed in step 5; the given circle Gamma reused as a free circle in three common-chord tricks). Under the same maximally-generous-reuse regime Ramanujan is 24-25. Quoting <=33 against its own 14 inflates the headline gap from 10-11 strokes to 19. Also 'expanded' is ambiguous — it suggests a collapsing-compass expansion, which is a different quantity from a faithful implementation count. ChatGPT still wins the fair head-to-head (14 vs 24-25), but the table's framing stacks the deck.

findings.claim: Q3: The stroke count is 14 with no double-counted or omitted strokes
findings.detail: Independent recount gives exactly 14 charged strokes: (1) c(P,PB), (2) c(B,PB), (3) line OW, (4) c(B,BO), (5) c(Q,QO), (6) chord line of Gamma & c(B,BO), (7) c(O,OX), (8) c(B,BX), (9) chord line of c(B,BX) & Gamma, (10) c(G,GO), (11) line OK, (12) c(X,XH), (13) chord line of c(X,XH) & c(O,OX), (14) line IW. Line OW, line OK, line IW and all three common-chord lines ARE charged; nothing is smuggled in as free. Points W,Q,X,G,K,H,H',I are all free intersections. The per-step tally 3+2+2+2+2+2+1=14 matches. Every stroke is load-bearing (no stroke's outputs go unused). Note the given diameter line P-O-B is used uncharged (X, G, I are 'meets OB') — legitimate ONLY because the diameter is a given, which ties Q3 to Q4.

findings.claim: Q3a (geometry): All claimed coordinates and the final identity IW^2 = (355/113) r^2 are correct
findings.detail: Verified numerically and in exact arithmetic: W=(0,sqrt(3)) (|PW|=|BW|=2), Q=(0,1), X=(1/2,0), G=(7/8,0) (power equality checks), K=(112/113, 98/113) exactly on both c(G,GO) and c(Q,QO) and outside Gamma, line OK has direction (8,7), H=(4/sqrt(113), 7/(2 sqrt(113))) with |OH|=1/2, |XH|^2 = 1/2 - 4/sqrt(113), the common chord of c(X,XH) & c(O,OX) is the vertical x = 4/sqrt(113) through H and its mirror H' (both confirmed on both circles, so the chord is a line through 2 constructed points), I=(4/sqrt(113),0), and IW^2 = 16/113 + 3 = 355/113 to machine precision. Relative side error vs sqrt(pi): +4.2457e-8, matching the claimed +4.246e-8 (same accuracy as Ramanujan 1913, both resting on the Milü 355/113).

findings.claim: Q4: The givens are identical across all three constructions
findings.detail: Ramanujan (circle, centre O, diameter PR) and ChatGPT (circle, center O, diameter P-O-B) are confirmed identical inventories, and ChatGPT genuinely consumes all of it: P and B for the equilateral circles, and the drawn diameter line for the three 'meets OB' intersections. However, the provided context never states the 15-move construction's givens. If the 15-move construction starts from less (e.g. circle + center only, no drawn diameter / no second endpoint), then '14 vs 15' compares unequal starting inventories and the one-stroke win could be an artifact — a drawn diameter with both endpoints is worth roughly 1-2 strokes of head start. This must be checked against the 15-move construction's spec before certifying the head-to-head.

findings.claim: Q5a: 14 vs 15 under the modern metric — genuinely one stroke shorter
findings.detail: Conditional on Q4 (identical givens), yes: 14 is a correct, honest count under the shared metric, one fewer than the audited 15-move construction, at identical accuracy sqrt(355/113) r.

findings.claim: Q5b: Zero-transfer property is real and is a genuine additional advantage under strict collapsing-compass rules
findings.detail: Audited all 8 circles: c(P,PB), c(B,PB), c(B,BO), c(Q,QO), c(O,OX), c(B,BX), c(G,GO), c(X,XH) — every one is centered at an endpoint of its own radius segment, i.e. directly drawable with a collapsing compass. Zero rigid transfers, so the count stays 14 under strict Euclid rules, versus the 15-move construction's 4 transfers expanding it to ~39-47. This is a decisive, verified advantage, arguably bigger than the 1-stroke headline.

findings.claim: Q5c: An obvious 13-stroke reduction exists
findings.detail: No reduction found after checking each stroke for redundancy. Every stroke's output is consumed. Attempted shortcuts all fail: (a) Q cannot be had without line OW — c(P,PB) and c(B,PB) are internally tangent to Gamma at B and P (verified), so they yield no new points on Gamma; (b) replacing c(Q,QO) by c(W,WO) (skipping OW and Q) gives common-chord slope sqrt(3)-dependent, not 7/8; the chord-slope mechanism (chord of two circles through O centered at (a1,b1),(a2,b2) is (a1-a2)x+(b1-b2)y=0) needs a center pair with difference direction (7,-8), and no cheaper constructible pair than {Q,G} was found; (c) replacing H by OK ∩ c(X,XO) gives |OH|=8/sqrt(113), x=64/113 — wrong constant; (d) the midpoint X and the perpendicular-foot I already use the cheapest known idioms (2 strokes each via chord reuse of Gamma and c(O,OX)). The construction is tight. Could not attempt a 14-stroke reduction of the 15-move construction — its step list is not in the provided context.

comparison.summary: ChatGPT's 14-stroke construction is geometrically correct end-to-end (all coordinates, the power equalities, K=(112/113,98/113), |OH|=r/2, and IW^2=(355/113)r^2 verified in exact/numeric arithmetic; side error +4.2457e-8 as claimed). The stroke count of 14 is honest — all lines including OW, OK, IW and the three common-chord lines are charged, no stroke is double-counted, and every stroke is load-bearing; no 13-stroke reduction was found. The metric is effectively the same as the reference metric, and the zero-transfer claim is real: all 8 circles are collapsing-compass legal, so 14 stands unchanged under strict Euclid rules vs ~39-47 for the 15-move construction — a verified, decisive advantage. Two caveats keep this from a clean 'valid': (1) the 'Ramanujan expanded <= 33' figure, while inside the faithful band (31-38), is not the fair comparator against ChatGPT's maximally-reused 14 — the apples-to-apples number is Ramanujan at 24-25; (2) the givens are confirmed identical only between ChatGPT and Ramanujan (circle + center + drawn diameter with both endpoints); the 15-move construction's givens are absent from the provided context, and since ChatGPT's construction leans on the drawn diameter (three 'meets OB' points) and both endpoints P,B, the 14-vs-15 head-to-head is valid only if the 15-move construction enjoyed the same starting inventory.
